teaching_arc.entry_point: Start from the question of what a robot program should do when the world does not match the happy path.
teaching_arc.progression: Introduce the branching tools, show how they change flow, then practice reading decisions in context.
teaching_arc.destination: Students should finish with a mental model for when and why branching is necessary.

audience_level: undergraduate industrial robotics students preparing for labs, robot selection, and basic programming decisions
topic_goal: Teach students how branching turns a linear robot program into decision-making logic that reacts to conditions.
topic_takeaways: Students should distinguish unconditional flow from condition-based flow.
Students should understand how branching supports error handling, sensing, and process variation.
Students should be able to read and reason about simple Fanuc branching logic.
style_notes: Keep the tone practical and shop-floor oriented.
Translate definitions into robot-cell decisions quickly.
Prefer concrete industrial examples over abstract theory when possible.
Keep pointing back to real triggers for branches: sensors, errors, and process choices.
Slow down enough that students can follow the flow change mentally.
transition_style: Move from the industrial question students would ask on the job to the next specification, control, or programming choice they need to make.

slides.narration_seed: Goal: students can read branching code confidently and implement Lab 8 tasks safely.
slides.transition_from_previous: Open by framing the topic goal: Teach students how branching turns a linear robot program into decision-making logic that reacts to conditions.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Today so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Frame Branching Instructions (FANUC TP) as the question or capability the rest of the topic is going to answer.
slides.story_hint: Keep Branching Instructions (FANUC TP) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Branching Instructions (FANUC TP) sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slides.narration_seed: Use Today to preview the route of the lesson, explain how this section fits the overall teaching arc, and tell students what to listen for next.
must_cover: Why branching matters in robotics
Two types: Unconditional vs Conditional
Unconditional: JMP/LBL, CALL, WAIT (time)
Conditional: WAIT (condition), IF, SELECT, FOR/ENDFOR
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Branching Instructions (FANUC TP) into Today so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into What is branching? so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students may watch the map update without understanding which signals are driving the update.
slides.explanation_style: Use Today to orient students, preview the route, and tell them what to listen for next.
slides.story_hint: Keep Today connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Today sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slides.narration_seed: Define What is branching? in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Branching lets you execute different sequences based on conditions
It uses instructions like IF, SELECT, ELSE to control program flow
In real cells, branching is used for:
• part present / no part • choose bin • retry logic • safety interlocks
slides.transition_from_previous: Use What is branching? to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once What is branching? is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define What is branching? in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep What is branching? connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain What is branching? accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Define Two categories of branching in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Unconditional branching (always changes flow)
• JMP/LBL • CALL • WAIT (time delay)
Conditional branching (changes flow only if condition is satisfied)
• WAIT (condition) • IF • SELECT • FOR/ENDFOR
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Two categories of branching to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Two categories of branching is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
likely_student_confusion: Students may watch the map update without understanding which signals are driving the update.
slides.explanation_style: Define Two categories of branching in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Two categories of branching connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Two categories of branching accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slide_ref.source_title: JMP / LBL (jump inside the same program)

slides.narration_seed: Define JMP / LBL (jump inside the same program) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Use LBL[n] to mark a location
Use JMP LBL[n] to jump to that location
Important: JMP/LBL can jump only within the same program (not to another program)
Common use: loops, retry blocks, skip sections

slides.narration_seed: Turn Quiz: JMP/LBL limitation into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: You want to jump from Program A into Program B using JMP. What is correct?
JMP/LBL is for flow inside the same TP program. Use CALL for other programs/macros.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Quiz: JMP/LBL limitation and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.

slide_ref.source_title: CALL (run another TP program or macro)

slides.narration_seed: Define CALL (run another TP program or macro) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: CALL runs another program/subroutine from the current program
You can call TP programs and macros (ex: a home routine)
Best practice: avoid deep nesting (programs calling programs calling programs)
Reason: nested calls make troubleshooting and tracking flow harder
slides.transition_from_previous: Use CALL (run another TP program or macro) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.

slides.narration_seed: Define WAIT (time-specified delay) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Two common forms:
Use when you need time for: clamp settle, air blow-off, sensor stabilization
Don’t overuse WAIT: it can hide real problems (like missing sensors)

slide_ref.source_title: WAIT edge conditions: On+ and Off-

slides.narration_seed: Define WAIT edge conditions: On+ and Off- in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: On+ (rising edge): condition becomes true when signal changes OFF → ON
Off- (falling edge): condition becomes true when signal changes ON → OFF
Use edge waits when you need to detect a transition (a moment), not a steady state
slides.transition_from_previous: Use WAIT edge conditions: On+ and Off- to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once WAIT edge conditions: On+ and Off- is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.narration_seed: Turn Quiz: level vs edge WAIT into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: You want the robot to continue only when a sensor changes from OFF to ON (a rising edge). Best WAIT form?
On+ detects the transition OFF→ON. = ON is a level condition (could already be ON).
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Quiz: level vs edge WAIT and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.

slides.story_hint: Tell students to imagine they are reviewing a robot cell or lab task and need to defend their answer to a teammate.

slides.narration_seed: Compare the alternatives or concepts on IF instruction (conditional compare) clearly, emphasize the trade-off, and end with the decision rule students should remember.
must_cover: IF executes processing only if the condition is true
Two main sources of conditions:
• Register compare: IF R[i] ...
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
• I/O compare: IF DI[i] = ON ...
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from Sample Lab 8 program (WAIT on DI then move) into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on IF instruction (conditional compare) explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of IF instruction (conditional compare) and know which side fits which situation.

slides.narration_seed: Treat Caution: comparing real values with = as a practical caution: explain the risk, what students often underestimate, and the safe operating rule they should remember.
must_cover: Avoid: IF R[1] = 1.5, ... for real values
Prefer ranges, for example:
Or compare against integer values when possible
State the consequence of getting this wrong, then give the clear safe rule.
must_say: Do not treat this as optional; treat it as an operating rule.
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Example: run different actions based on registers into Caution: comparing real values with = so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Multiple conditions with AND / OR so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Explain Caution: comparing real values with = like an operating warning: state the risk, the consequence, and the safe rule.
slides.story_hint: Use a believable lab or shop-floor mistake rather than abstract warning language.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should feel the practical consequence of getting Caution: comparing real values with = wrong and remember the safe rule.

slides.narration_seed: Define Multiple conditions with AND / OR in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: You can combine multiple conditions on one line using:
Rules from the lab manual:
• up to five conditions can be combined on a single line
• mixing AND and OR together is prohibited (readability + editing safety)

slides.narration_seed: Turn Quiz: AND/OR mixing into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: Which line is NOT allowed by the controller rules described in the lab manual?
The manual prohibits using AND and OR in combination on the same instruction line. Split into multiple IF lines.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.

slides.narration_seed: Define SELECT instruction (switch/case for registers) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: It compares R[i] to multiple values and executes the first matching action
Structure:
Actions are typically CALL or JMP LBL

slide_ref.source_title: Important SELECT caution: first match wins

slides.narration_seed: Treat Important SELECT caution: first match wins as a practical caution: explain the risk, what students often underestimate, and the safe operating rule they should remember.
must_cover: Example scenario:
Result: PROG2 runs; PROG4 does NOT run (even though it matches too)
State the consequence of getting this wrong, then give the clear safe rule.
must_say: Do not treat this as optional; treat it as an operating rule.

must_cover: In a SELECT block, R[1]=2 and you have two cases for =2. What happens?
SELECT executes the first matching statement and skips the rest, even if other cases match.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.

Two directions:
• TO (count up) • DOWNTO (count down)

slide_ref.source_title: When does a FOR loop run (important rules)?

slides.narration_seed: Define When does a FOR loop run (important rules)? in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: When TO is used: initial value must be ≤ target value
When DOWNTO is used: initial value must be ≥ target value
If the condition is not satisfied, the loop runs 0 times (skips to the line after ENDFOR)
FOR statement itself executes once to initialize the loop counter
slides.transition_from_previous: Use When does a FOR loop run (important rules)? to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once When does a FOR loop run (important rules)? is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define When does a FOR loop run (important rules)? in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep When does a FOR loop run (important rules)? connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain When does a FOR loop run (important rules)? accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Define What ENDFOR does each cycle in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: If TO is used: loop counter is incremented each iteration
If DOWNTO is used: loop counter is decremented each iteration
Loop repeats while:
• TO: counter < target
slides.transition_from_previous: Use What ENDFOR does each cycle to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once What ENDFOR does each cycle is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define What ENDFOR does each cycle in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep What ENDFOR does each cycle connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain What ENDFOR does each cycle accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slide_ref.source_title: Example: repeat a move sequence 5 times

slides.narration_seed: Define Example: repeat a move sequence 5 times in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Note from manual: internal loop delay exists → WAIT is usually not required to “slow down” the loop.

slides.narration_seed: Define FOR/ENDFOR pairing + common alarm in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: FANUC automatically combines the closest FOR with the closest ENDFOR
If you have a FOR without a matching ENDFOR → execution alarm
Example alarm name from manual:
Always ensure number of FOR statements equals number of ENDFOR statements
slides.transition_from_previous: Use FOR/ENDFOR pairing + common alarm to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once FOR/ENDFOR pairing + common alarm is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define FOR/ENDFOR pairing + common alarm in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep FOR/ENDFOR pairing + common alarm connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain FOR/ENDFOR pairing + common alarm accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Define Nested loops (FOR inside FOR) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: You can teach a FOR/ENDFOR inside another FOR/ENDFOR
Up to 10 nested loops are supported (more can raise an execution alarm)
Important safety note from manual:
Do not use the same register as loop counters inside the same nested loop
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Nested loops (FOR inside FOR) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Nested loops (FOR inside FOR) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slides.narration_seed: Define Nested loops example (2 loops) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Closest pairing rule: inner FOR pairs with inner ENDFOR first
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Nested loops example (2 loops) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.

slides.narration_seed: Turn Quiz: iteration loop into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: What happens if you run: FOR R[1] = 10 TO 1 (TO but initial > target)?
Rule: with TO, initial must be ≤ target. If not, the loop is not executed and control jumps past ENDFOR.
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Quiz: iteration loop and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.
likely_student_confusion: Students may rush to the answer on Quiz: iteration loop without first stating the reasoning steps.
slides.explanation_style: Turn Quiz: iteration loop into a guided decision: pause for thought, then reveal the reasoning step by step.
slides.story_hint: Tell students to imagine they are reviewing a robot cell or lab task and need to defend their answer to a teammate.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Quiz: iteration loop question on their own and explain the reasoning.

slide_ref.source_title: Where are these instructions on the pendant?

must_cover: General flow:
Categories you will use today:
Use Double Display so you can watch registers and I/O while editing/running

slides.narration_seed: Turn Best practices (keep your program readable) into a short guided practice moment: let students think first, then explain the reasoning in a teacher-like way.
must_cover: Prefer SELECT over many IFs when cases are mutually exclusive
Avoid mixing AND/OR in one IF line → split into multiple IF lines
Avoid deep CALL nesting (hard to debug)
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
Use labels carefully: name/comment blocks so you remember what LBL means
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Best practices (keep your program readable) and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.
likely_student_confusion: Students may rush to the answer on Best practices (keep your program readable) without first stating the reasoning steps.
slides.explanation_style: Turn Best practices (keep your program readable) into a guided decision: pause for thought, then reveal the reasoning step by step.
slides.story_hint: Tell students to imagine they are reviewing a robot cell or lab task and need to defend their answer to a teammate.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Best practices (keep your program readable) question on their own and explain the reasoning.

slides.narration_seed: Use Quick self-check as a final self-check so students can tell whether they now understand the core ideas of the topic.
must_cover: Can you explain the difference between WAIT 1.0 sec and WAIT DI[101]=ON?
Can you choose between JMP vs CALL correctly?
Can you write an IF with 2 conditions using AND (no OR)?
Students should distinguish unconditional flow from condition-based flow.
Do you understand why SELECT only runs the first matching case?
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Quick self-check to consolidate what the last few slides have built up and turn it into memory.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Questions? so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Use Quick self-check as a final self-check so students leave knowing what they should now be able to explain or do.
slides.story_hint: Keep Quick self-check connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should leave Quick self-check with the main takeaways of the topic compressed into usable memory.

slides.narration_seed: Define Questions? in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Common issues:
• WAIT condition never becomes true (DI simulation not enabled)
• Missing ENDFOR alarm (INTP-670)
• SELECT doesn’t run the second matching line (first-match behavior)
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Questions? to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Close by restating the destination of the topic: Students should finish with a mental model for when and why branching is necessary.
